itineraries.title: Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From resort beaches in the north to wild southern coasts - lagoons, forests, cliffs, and Creole heritage in a clockwise loop.
itineraries.notes: Clockwise circuit from north to southwest. 4 accommodation changes over 10 days. Want more visits and activities in certain locations, or to discover Port Louis, the Tea Route, and the central plateau? Check out our 'Mauritius in Depth' 15-day circuit

visits.text: Trou aux Biches is the perfect choice for an easy start. The lagoon is protected by a coral reef - the water is flat, crystal-clear, and you can stand far from shore. Arrive mid-morning when the sunlight hits the white sand just right.

Mont Choisy is the longest beach in the north: nearly 2 km lined with shade-giving casuarina trees. Less crowded than Trou aux Biches on weekdays - a solid option if you're looking for some breathing room.

activities.text: Snorkeling straight from the beach at Trou aux Biches - sea turtles regularly frequent this area. No boat needed. Bring your mask and snorkel if you have them; local operators rent gear on-site.

Sunset from La Cuvette, the small cove at Grand Baie. This is where locals gather at the end of the day - the vibe is authentic, the orientation due west. It's about 15 minutes on foot from Grand Baie town centre.

visits.text: Cap Malheureux owes its name to the shipwrecks that littered this wind-battered headland. The Notre-Dame Auxiliatrice church - red roof, white walls - is one of the most photographed in Mauritius. Facing it, Coin de Mire rises on the horizon about 18 km offshore.

Pereybere is a pocket beach with a different vibe from Grand Baie - more local, less touristy. Vendors selling gâteaux piments walk along the waterfront late morning. Make the walk from Cap Malheureux on foot; it's 10 minutes. Bain Boeuf is 5 minutes further along the coast - a quiet cove, almost tourist-free.

activities.text: Option A - if you haven't done the catamaran yet: afternoon swim at Pereybère or Bain Boeuf, local atmosphere, no resort in sight. Option B - if you prefer to dedicate this entire day to the islets: catamaran excursion to Coin de Mire, Île Plate, and Îlot Gabriel (about 6 hours, lunch on board, snorkeling around Îlot Gabriel). If going this route, book the day before and depart in the morning directly from Grand Baie harbour - Cap Malheureux and Pereybère can be done in 1 hour the evening before.

map_keywords: Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam Botanical Garden Pamplemousses
L'Aventure du Sucre Beau Plan Mauritius

visits.text: The Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam Botanical Garden in Pamplemousses is one of the oldest in the tropics - founded in 1770 under Pierre Poivre, administrator of the Îles de France. It's home to over 650 species including giant Victoria amazonica water lilies and talipot palms, which flower only once in 60 years before dying. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

L'Aventure du Sucre in Beau Plan is a museum housed in a 19th-century sugar factory. It tells the story of sugarcane - and of the slavery that made it possible. The period machinery is still in place. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours.

Château de Labourdonnais in Mapou is a colonial mansion built in 1859 - one of the few in the country still habitable. The estate includes an orchard with 70 fruit species and a craft rum distillery. The guided tour lasts about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Tasting of rum and artisanal jams at Château de Labourdonnais. The house-made flavoured rums use fruit from the orchard - it's a short but pleasant stop at the end of the tour.

visits.text: Île aux Cerfs is reached from Trou d'Eau Douce by boat - a 10-minute crossing. The island belongs to a hotel complex but beach access is public. Take time to walk along the eastern shore: the sand is white, the lagoon a vivid blue, and crowds concentrate mainly around the central snack bars - just walk 10 minutes to find space.

The lagoon on the northwest side of Île aux Cerfs is the calmest and most photogenic - shallow waters, white sand bottom, corals visible to the naked eye. This is where glass-bottom boat tours operate. Operators offer short rotations of about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Snorkeling in the Île aux Cerfs lagoon - visibility is excellent and coral beds are accessible without tanks. Operators also offer glass-bottom boat tours around the island.
Return to Trou d'Eau Douce late afternoon. Take a walk along the waterfront - it's a quiet fishing village with painted boats moored along the wharf. A good hour before dinner.

map_keywords: Vallee de Ferney Nature Reserve Mauritius

visits.text: Belle Mare is one of the longest beaches in Mauritius - about 9 km of continuous white sand. Fewer resorts than on the west coast, more space. Go early morning to catch the low-angle light on the water.

The Vallée de Ferney Nature Reserve protects one of the island's last endemic forests. You may spot the Mauritius kestrel - a falcon that nearly went extinct in the 1970s and whose population recovered thanks to a captive breeding programme. The main trail takes 2 hours return.

activities.text: Flacq Market if you're there Wednesday or Sunday - it's one of the liveliest open-air markets on the island. Spices, vegetables, fabrics, fish: all of everyday Mauritian life converges here. Allow 1 hour to wander.

If it's not a market day: drive along the coast between Pointe de Flacq and Roches Noires - near-deserted beaches, fishermen mending their nets, no tourists. This is the authentic east, 15 minutes from Belle Mare.

visits.text: Mahébourg is the most authentic village in the southeast - a waterfront without hotels, Creole houses, old fishermen on the wharves. The National History Museum recounts the Battle of Grand Port in August 1810: Napoleon's only naval victory against the British, it's inscribed on the Arc de Triomphe in Paris. The visit takes about 45 minutes.

Blue Bay has been classified as a marine park since 2000 - one of the best-preserved coral reefs in the western Indian Ocean. Operators offer glass-bottom boat tours that let you see the corals without getting wet. Allow 1 hour for the outing.
Pointe d'Esny is a quiet beach 3 km from Blue Bay - less crowded, lined with casuarinas, with a clear, shallow lagoon. Perfect for a calm swim late afternoon.

activities.text: Snorkeling in Blue Bay Marine Park - branching corals, surgeonfish, leopard rays. The protected zone starts right at the surface. If you're a beginner, stay near shore; the seabed is readable from 1 to 2 metres down.
Mahébourg market on Monday morning - fruit, spices, fresh fish of the day. It's a short stop but it gives a good pulse of the village. If you arrive Sunday evening, plan your Day 6 accordingly.

map_keywords: La Roche qui Pleure Souillac Mauritius

visits.text: Gris-Gris is one of the few places in Mauritius without a coral barrier - the Indian Ocean strikes the basalt rock directly. Waves throw spray several metres high. Don't climb down onto wet rocks. La Roche qui Pleure is 5 minutes on foot: water permanently seeps down the cliff face, creating a weeping effect - a geological curiosity.

Rochester Falls are located 4 km from Souillac, accessible by a dirt track. The waterfall cascades over basalt columns with perfectly straight angles - a rare volcanic formation that gives the impression of manmade architecture. Drop height: about 10 metres. Wear closed shoes.

The Macondé viewpoint offers one of the most spectacular coastal views in Mauritius - the cliff plunges down to Baie du Cap, and on clear days you can make out Île aux Bénitiers in the distance. It's on the road between the south coast and Chamarel: don't miss it even if you're tired. Parking on the roadside, 2 minutes' walk.

activities.text: Road trip along the southern coastal road - from Souillac to Baie du Cap, the road follows wind-battered cliffs, abandoned cane fields, and fishing villages without tourists. Take your time, stop at random. This is the most 'road' day of the circuit: savouring the movement is part of the programme.

Climb toward Chamarel via the switchbacks - the road rises quickly from the coast to the island's highlands. The bends offer sea views at every turn. Arrive late afternoon: settle in, and eat local tonight.

map_keywords: Terre des Sept Couleurs Chamarel Mauritius

visits.text: The Seven Coloured Earths of Chamarel are a geological curiosity formed by the decomposition of volcanic basalt into clays of different colours - ochre, brick red, violet, khaki green. The dunes are about 100 metres in diameter. What's remarkable is that the colours don't mix even in the rain. The visit takes 30 minutes.

Chamarel Falls plunge about a hundred metres into the gorge below. They're among the highest in Mauritius. The viewing platform is accessible from the Seven Coloured Earths entrance - both visits are done together.

The Rhumerie de Chamarel produces the island's only agricultural rums - and the only arabica coffee grown in Mauritius. Visit the plantations, follow the distillation process, finish with a tasting. Allow 1 hour. If Mauritian coffee intrigues you, this is the only place.

Ebony Forest is a private reserve protecting a forest of endemic black ebony - a critically endangered species. The trail to Point Sublime (about 1 km return) opens onto a 180° view of Le Morne and the west coast. Leave early morning for the best light.

activities.text: Descent to Le Morne mid-afternoon - 15 minutes' drive from Chamarel. The beach at Le Morne opens onto a perfectly calm lagoon on the north side. Swimming, hammock, or simply watching Le Morne Brabant from the water.

visits.text: Le Morne Brabant rises to 556 metres and has been UNESCO-listed since 2008 - not for its geology, but for what it represents: in the 18th and 19th centuries, fugitive slaves took refuge in its inaccessible caves. Legend has it that they threw themselves off the cliffs on the day of the abolition of slavery in 1835, believing the soldiers who came to announce it were there to arrest them. The guided ascent takes 3 to 4 hours return. A guide is mandatory to reach the summit - book the day before.

activities.text: Kitesurfing in the Le Morne lagoon - it's one of the world's most renowned spots. The southeast trade wind blows steadily from May to November, and the lagoon is shallow with no current. Operators offer beginner courses. If you don't kitesurf, watching the riders from the beach is already worth something.
Sunset facing Île aux Bénitiers from Le Morne beach - the islet silhouettes to the west, the sky turns orange then red. This is the last proper evening of the circuit. Take it in.

map_keywords: Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam International Airport Mauritius

visits.text: Tamarin Bay is one of the few places in the world where spinner and humpback dolphins live year-round. Departures leave around 6:30 am from the wharves - dolphins are active early morning. Return by 8:30-9:00 am. This isn't a staged attraction: you're on a fishing boat, and the dolphins are there or they're not.

activities.text: Last-minute shopping in Quatre Bornes if you have time - the town is on the route between Tamarin and the airport. The textile market and shopping galleries are open from 9 am. Allow 30 to 45 minutes if you have purchases to make.
Drive to SSR airport via the interior - about 1 hour 15 minutes from Tamarin. No particular difficulty, the road is well signposted. Plan to arrive 2.5 hours before your flight.

seo.h1_title: Mauritius Circuit: The Essentials in 10 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: This circuit loops around Mauritius clockwise - resort beaches in the north, wild eastern shores, clifftop south, volcanic southwest. In 10 days, you'll stay in 4 different accommodations and cross landscapes that bear no resemblance to each other. From the lagoon at Grand Baie to the UNESCO-listed summit of Le Morne Brabant, the island reveals its full diversity.
seo.seo_hook: Northern beaches, eastern reefs, southern cliffs, Chamarel's coloured earths, and the memory of slavery at Le Morne - Mauritius in 10 days without backtracking.
featured_list: Grand Baie: ideal northern base for offshore islets and Mont Choisy beaches
Île aux Cerfs: deepwater lagoon, accessible by boat from Trou d'Eau Douce
Chamarel: seven-coloured volcanic dunes and a 100-metre waterfall
Le Morne Brabant: UNESCO-listed summit, symbol of the island's slave memory

places_taglines.MU::grand-baie: Where turquoise shallows meet offshore islands you can actually reach
places_taglines.MU::trou-deau-douce: A fishing village where painted boats set off for paradise lagoons
places_taglines.MU::mahebourg: Where Creole verandas overlook the island's only classified marine park
places_taglines.MU::chamarel: Where volcanic soil bleeds seven colours and waterfalls carve basalt gorges
places_taglines.MU::le-morne: A UNESCO mountain that holds the memory of those who chose freedom

highlights: Catamaran excursion to the northern islets from Grand Baie - Coin de Mire, Île Plate, and Îlot Gabriel
Guided ascent of Le Morne Brabant (556 m), UNESCO-listed since 2008 for the memory of maroon slaves
Blue Bay Marine Park - one of the best-preserved coral reefs in the western Indian Ocean
Sunrise dolphin outing in Tamarin Bay - year-round resident species

itineraries.title: Mauritius in Depth: 15 Days from the Interior to the Coast
itineraries.subtitle: Central plateau, Tea Route, endemic forests, wild south, and beach rewards – the complete journey to truly understand Mauritius.
itineraries.notes: This itinerary spirals from the interior highlands out to the coasts. Six accommodation changes over 15 days. The central plateau (Curepipe, Moka) runs significantly cooler and wetter than the coasts – pack a light sweater. Want more visits and activities at some of these places, or a shorter first glimpse? Check out our 'Essential Mauritius' 10-day circuit.

visits.text: Get to the Central Market by 7am – that's when the stalls overflow with spices, tropical fruits, and fresh street food. Try the dholl puri, a split-pea-stuffed flatbread that's been sold on Port Louis streets for generations. Mine frit, gâteau piment, bol renversé: the market delivers the best meal of the day, and the cheapest.

Aapravasi Ghat has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2006. This wharf is where, starting in 1834, the first indentured laborers from India landed to replace enslaved workers after abolition. Between 1834 and 1924, nearly 500,000 people passed through. Plan for 45 minutes – the site is small but heavy with history.

The Blue Penny Museum holds two of the world's rarest stamps: the One Penny and Two Pence from 1847, the first stamps issued in Mauritius. They're only displayed in dim light to prevent deterioration. The museum also traces the island's history from the Portuguese onward – allow an hour.

Chinatown spreads around Rue Royale – a few blocks of pagodas, grocery stores, and Cantonese restaurants that recall Mauritius's waves of Chinese immigration since the 19th century. Not a polished tourist quarter: a genuine neighborhood enclave, with its smells and corner shops.

activities.text: Caudan Waterfront occupies renovated colonial warehouses facing the harbor. It's the most open-air corner of Port Louis – a solid option for a shaded lunch before the afternoon heat.

Climb up to Fort Adelaide – La Citadelle – late in the day. Built by the British between 1834 and 1840, it overlooks the city from 100 meters up. Late afternoon light on the harbor and Port Louis rooftops is the best you'll get all day. Bring water: the walk up from the center is steep.

visits.text: Eureka has 109 doors – yes, 109. This 1830 Creole house in Moka is the largest on the island, built in the typical colonial Mascarene style: verandas on three sides, camphor wood, period furniture. Wander through the rooms, look at the yellowed photographs, then head down to the garden – it follows a river where you can swim.

Trou aux Cerfs is a volcanic crater 300 meters across and 85 meters deep right in the heart of Curepipe. A path circles it in less than a kilometer. On clear days, you get the central plateau mountain chain on one side – and sometimes both coasts of the island.

Curepipe is Mauritius's wettest town, perched at 550 meters altitude. Misty in the morning, cool (18-22°C), it has the atmosphere of a colonial mountain town – wooden houses, covered market, slow rhythm. Pack a light sweater for the evening.

activities.text: If you're there on a Thursday or Sunday, detour to the Quatre Bornes market – the island's big textile market. Hundreds of stalls selling clothes, sheets, shoes at rock-bottom prices. Not touristy at all: this is where Mauritians do their shopping.

visits.text: Domaine des Aubineaux is a late-19th-century colonial house with Italian frescoes painted on the ceilings and intact period furniture. In the garden, a colophane tree is over 500 years old – one of the oldest trees on the island. Admission is often included in Tea Route combo tickets.

Bois Chéri has been producing tea since 1892 – it's the island's first and oldest plantation. Tour the factory to understand the transformation process from leaf to cup. Then head up to the panoramic restaurant: the view over the south coast on clear days is worth the trip alone. Try the vanilla tea and coconut tea – they're the house signatures. ⚠️ The factory only runs tours certain days of the week: check before you go.

Saint Aubin Estate: a sugar plantation since 1819, one of Mauritius's oldest. It's here that the island's first agricultural rum is produced. The Vanilla House walks you through the entire transformation chain – from the Vanilla planifolia orchid to the dried pod. Allow 1.5 hours to tour the estate.

activities.text: This is a day for the palate. Flavored tea tastings at Bois Chéri, aged rum and cane punch at Saint Aubin, fresh vanilla scraped on the spot. Leave space in your luggage – the teas and rums from these estates aren't as easy to find elsewhere.

visits.text: Grand Bassin – Ganga Talao in Creole – is a crater lake at 550 meters altitude surrounded by multicolored Hindu temples. A 33-meter statue of Shiva greets you at the entrance. It's the most important pilgrimage site in Mauritius for the Hindu community. In February-March, during Maha Shivaratri, hundreds of thousands of pilgrims walk barefoot from across the island – sometimes 40 kilometers on foot to reach the lake.

Black River Gorges National Park covers 6,574 hectares – it's the largest area of native forest still standing in Mauritius. The Macchabée trails cut through canopy forest with tree ferns and rare endemic fauna. Allow 3 to 5 hours depending on your pace. Pack water, closed-toe shoes, and sunscreen.

Plaine Champagne is a 700-meter-high plateau with moorland landscapes unlike anything else in Mauritius. Alexandra Falls plunges into the gorges from this plateau – the viewpoint over the falls and the forest below is worth the 20-minute stop.

activities.text: Physical day – arrive in Chamarel late afternoon and settle in. The village is quiet, accommodations often tucked deep in the greenery. Good evening to do nothing at all.

visits.text: Arrive at the Seven Coloured Earth right at opening at 8:30am. After 10am, tour buses roll in and it's over. These volcanic earth dunes owe their colors to basalt decomposing into clays: iron oxides give the red and brown, aluminum oxides the blue and purple. The phenomenon is real – the colors never mix, even after torrential rain.

Chamarel Falls drops nearly 100 meters – it's the island's highest waterfall. The viewpoint is accessible from the Seven Coloured Earth site. It's more impressive during the rainy season (December-March), but visible year-round.

Chamarel Rum Distillery is the only arabica coffee operation in Mauritius, grown on volcanic soil at 300 meters altitude. It also produces rum – white rum, aged rum, spiced rum. Plan for the tasting: it's included in the tour and the bottles aren't available everywhere.

activities.text: Ebony Forest is a private reserve of native ebony forest with 140 plant species. The trail climbs to Point Sublime – a plunging view over Le Morne and the entire west coast. Allow 1.5 hours round trip. It's one of the rare chances to see what Mauritius looked like before deforestation.

visits.text: Le Morne Brabant rises to 556 meters and has been a UNESCO site since 2008. This massive rock jutting into the sea served as refuge for maroon slaves in the 18th and 19th centuries. Legend says that when slavery was abolished in 1835, police climbed up to tell them – and seeing them approach, the slaves thought they were coming to capture them and threw themselves off the cliff. A guide is mandatory to reach the summit. Plan for 3 to 4 hours round trip.

The Slave Route Monument stands at the foot of Le Morne, facing the sea. It recalls that this peninsula was one of the last refuges for escaped slaves in Mauritius. Stark, almost excessively so – a few minutes suffice, but they count.

activities.text: Le Morne beach stretches at the foot of the rock, facing the lagoon. After the climb, this is where you land for the rest of the afternoon. The water is calm, the bottom white sand, and the view of Le Morne Brabant from the sea is the finest on the island.

map_keywords: Cathedral Dive Site Flic en Flac

visits.text: Flic en Flac is the big beach on the west coast – several kilometers of sand, clear water, sunset facing the sea. Less hidden than Le Morne, but much longer. Cathedral Dive Site, two kilometers offshore, features underwater arches at 30 meters depth – for certified divers only.

Casela Nature Parks sits in the foothills of the Black River Range, between Le Morne and Flic en Flac. Zipline, quad, buggy safari, interaction with lions and giraffes: it's an animal and adventure park that works as well for families as for adults. Allow half a day.

activities.text: Depart at 6:30am for the dolphin outing in Tamarin Bay – 15 minutes from Le Morne. Spinner dolphins and Indo-Pacific humpback dolphins are resident in the bay and visible almost year-round at sunrise. Local operators run these trips on rigid inflatable boats. Get there early: the dolphins dive as soon as the heat rises.

visits.text: At Gris-Gris, forget the lagoon. Here the ocean pounds straight onto the rock – no coral barrier to slow it down. Wind blows, waves explode on the black cliffs. You'd think you were in Brittany, not the tropics. 200 meters away, La Roche qui Pleure is a cliff where water constantly seeps through by filtration in the volcanic rock.

Rochester Falls is 4 kilometers from Souillac. What makes this waterfall unique are its perfectly geometric rectangular basalt columns – as if someone had stacked bricks of black rock. Swimming possible in the pool below. Take the path from the parking lot: 10 minutes on foot.

La Vanille Nature Park is home to Aldabra giant tortoises – some over 100 years old and 200 kilos. There are also Nile crocodiles and an insectarium. It's not a spectacular animal park, but the tortoises you can approach and touch are worth the trip, especially with children.

Bel Ombre is a former sugar estate converted in the southwest of the island, with preserved nature and hiking trails in the hills. Not essential, but if you pass through, the nature reserve adjoining the estate gives an idea of what Mauritius looked like before sugarcane.

activities.text: Road day through the wild south. Take the coast, stop when you want – it's a day of mileage and discovery. Pack a picnic or eat at a small restaurant in Souillac: the village is calm, authentic, no tourists.

visits.text: The National History Museum in Mahébourg occupies an early-19th-century colonial house. It traces the Battle of Grand Port in 1810 – Napoleon's only naval victory against the British. The name "Grand Port" is engraved on the Arc de Triomphe in Paris, among the great battles of the Empire. Allow an hour. On Mondays, the Mahébourg market sets up in front: fruit, spices, crafts – arrive early.

Blue Bay is a protected marine park with one of the best-preserved coral reefs on the island. Snorkeling from the beach or glass-bottom boat excursion to see corals without getting wet. The water is shallow – 2 to 4 meters in places – making it one of the best snorkeling spots in Mauritius.
Pointe d'Esny is the beach next to Blue Bay, quieter, bordered by mangrove. A handful of small Creole houses, few people. If Blue Bay is crowded, retreat here.

activities.text: Mahébourg is an authentic fishing village with a waterfront where locals gather at day's end. No tourist commerce, no resort. A good evening to eat grilled fish at a small local restaurant and watch the sea.

map_keywords: Île aux Cerfs Trou d'Eau Douce

visits.text: Île aux Cerfs is reached by a 10-minute boat from Trou d'Eau Douce. Full day on site: white sand beaches, turquoise lagoon, snorkeling, kayaking. The site is very busy – leave at 9am with the first catamarans if you want a quiet beach in the morning. Avoid weekends if possible.

activities.text: In the evening, Trou d'Eau Douce is a no-frills little fishing port. A few fish restaurants on the waterfront, boats coming in at sunset. It's an ordinary Mauritian village – after a day on Île aux Cerfs, it's exactly what you need.

visits.text: Ferney Valley nature reserve protects an endemic forest where the Mauritius kestrel lives – one of the world's rarest raptors. In the 1970s, only 4 individuals remained. Thanks to a conservation program, the population now exceeds 400. Reserve guides take you on trails to observe them.

Belle Mare is one of the longest beaches on the island – several kilometers of fine white sand on the east side. The water here is calmer than on the south coast. Less crowded than Île aux Cerfs, more space. Plan the afternoon here.

activities.text: The coastal villages of the east – Trou d'Eau Douce, Pointe de Flacq – have kept a quiet fishing atmosphere. An hour's walk is enough to feel the rhythm of Mauritius's east coast, far from the northern resorts.

map_keywords: Île d'Ambre kayak Rivière La Chaux

visits.text: Île d'Ambre is reached by kayak from Rivière La Chaux, at the northeast tip. Allow 2 to 3 hours for an excursion through the mangrove, with native forest and ruins of an old colonial quarantine station on the island. Operators offer these guided trips. No postcard beach – raw, little-visited nature.

Roches Noires: Mauritius's northeast coast is the island's least touristy. Black basalt, rough sea, constant wind. Poste Lafayette is a wild beach with wind and few people. Not a calm swimming beach – a coast for watching the sea.

Notre-Dame Auxiliatrice Church at Cap Malheureux has a bright red roof that stands out against the sky and sea – it's the most photographed image of Mauritius. Across the water, on clear days, you see Coin de Mire distinctly. A five-minute stop is enough, but evening light is best.

activities.text: The northeast coast is raw Mauritius – no resort, no polished lagoon. A good half-day's drive on small coastal roads between fishing villages. Plan to buy fresh fish at one of the roadside markets late in the day.

visits.text: Sir Seewoosagur Ramgoolam Botanical Garden in Pamplemousses was founded in 1770 by Pierre Poivre, a French administrator who acclimatized spices from around the world here – cinnamon, nutmeg, clove – to break the Dutch monopoly. You'll see the giant Victoria amazonica water lilies, and if you're lucky, the talipot palm: it flowers once in 60 years, then dies. Over 650 plant species on 37 hectares.

L'Aventure du Sucre is housed in the old Beau Plan sugar mill. The museum traces the complete history of sugarcane in Mauritius: the beginnings of cultivation, slavery, Indian indenture, mechanization. Tasting of raw sugars in different colors and agricultural rums at the end. Allow 1.5 hours.

Château de Labourdonnais is a 19th-century colonial manor with an orchard of 1,500 fruit trees and a rum distillery. The guided tour includes the restored house and tasting. It's more intimate than L'Aventure du Sucre – fewer visitors, quieter setting.

activities.text: Late in the day, head to the northern beaches. Grand Baie is the liveliest town on this coast – restaurants, bars, shops. After 12 days of culture and nature, it's a welcome change of pace.

visits.text: Full-day catamaran excursion to the northern islets. Coin de Mire is a rock shaped like a gunstock – you can't land, it's a bird sanctuary, but you snorkel around it in crystal-clear waters. Île Plate: colonial lighthouse, snorkeling on the reefs. Îlot Gabriel: white sand beach, barbecue aboard, swimming. Return late afternoon. Book the day before – spots fill fast.

activities.text: Evening in Grand Baie: it's the nightlife capital of the north. Restaurants of all cuisines, bars on the waterfront, action till late. After 13 days of deep circuit touring, it's decompression evening. Enjoy.

visits.text: Last morning swim at Trou aux Biches or Mont Choisy – the two finest beaches in the north, 10-15 minutes from Grand Baie. Trou aux Biches is livelier, Mont Choisy wilder. Choose according to your morning mood.

activities.text: Last hour of shopping before the airport: Chamarel rum, vanilla tea from Bois Chéri, market spices, scale models of traditional Mauritian boats. SSR Airport is about 1h15 via the highway from Grand Baie – don't leave too late.

seo.h1_title: Mauritius in Depth: From the Interior to the Coasts
seo.intro_paragraph: This 15-day circuit explores Mauritius backwards from classic routes: first the misty central plateau, 19th-century tea plantations, and Black River gorges, before descending to the wild south and its volcanic cliffs. Beaches come in the second half – like a well-earned reward after days of forest, hiking, and terroir.
seo.seo_hook: Central plateau, endemic forests, wild south, deserted east coast, and northern islets: Mauritius for curious travelers who want to understand the island before they bronze on it.
featured_list: Aapravasi Ghat: UNESCO site where 500,000 indentured laborers landed starting in 1834
Black River Gorges National Park: 6,574 hectares of native forest, hikes up to 5 hours
Bois Chéri: Mauritius's first tea plantation since 1892, with panoramic restaurant
Le Morne Brabant: guided ascent of the maroon slaves' refuge rock, UNESCO 2008

places_taglines.MU::port-louis: Where centuries of migration converge in steaming market stalls and colonial harbors
places_taglines.MU::curepipe: A misty highland town perched at 550 meters, gateway to the Tea Route
places_taglines.MU::chamarel: Where volcanic earth bleeds seven colors and rhum flows from mountainside stills
places_taglines.MU::le-morne: A towering basalt sentinel that sheltered freedom seekers and now guards their memory
places_taglines.MU::mahebourg: A fishing village that witnessed Napoleon's only naval triumph over the British
places_taglines.MU::trou-deau-douce: A quiet harbor where fishermen's boats share the pier with turquoise-bound ferries
places_taglines.MU::cap-malheureux: Where a crimson church roof frames distant islets rising from cobalt waters
places_taglines.MU::grand-baie: The north coast's beating heart, where sunset catamarans return to buzzing waterfronts

highlights: Aapravasi Ghat: UNESCO site where nearly 500,000 indentured laborers transited between 1834 and 1924
Black River Gorges National Park: 6,574 hectares of endemic forest with 3 to 5 hour hikes
Bois Chéri: Mauritius's first tea plantation, operating since 1892, with panoramic views over the south coast
Mauritius kestrel at Ferney Valley: raptor saved from extinction in the 1970s, from 4 individuals to over 400 today
